preparation_method: served with spring onion and cucumber

serving.description: 1 full set (whole duck with pancakes, hoisin sauce, spring onion, cucumber)
serving.natural_language_portion: A full Peking Duck set typically comprises one whole roasted duck (approximately 1.0–1.2 kg edible yield) served with 20–24 thin wheat pancakes, hoisin sauce, sliced spring onion, and julienned cucumber, intended for 4–6 people sharing.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05141)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy entry for roasted duck (meat and skin), which represents the primary edible component of Peking Duck. The full-set accompaniments (thin wheat pancakes, hoisin sauce, spring onion, cucumber) contribute additional carbohydrates and sodium; per-100g values here reflect the composite edible portion of the full set estimated at approximately 60% roasted duck with skin and 40% pancakes and condiments. A blended per-100g estimate is used: duck with skin (USDA NDB 05141) weighted 60% and thin wheat pancake (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168936, Mandarin pancake proxy) weighted 40%, with hoisin sauce sodium contribution factored in. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Peking Duck full set. GI of 52 is estimated as a weighted composite: roasted duck meat has negligible GI contribution (protein/fat dominant, GI ~0); thin wheat pancakes (Mandarin-style, unleavened) are analogous to wheat flour flatbread with GI approximately 52–62 per University of Sydney GI Database (wheat flour tortilla GI=52, reference food glucose=100). The composite GI of 52 reflects the low-medium boundary driven by the wheat pancake component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 170.4g carbohydrate in 1200g serving) / 100 ≈ 24.3. Carbohydrate per serving = 14.2g per 100g × 1200g / 100 × 0.40 pancake fraction adjusted = approximately 46.8g net carbohydrate from pancakes; GL = 52 × 46.8 / 100 = 24.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from hoisin sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Peking Duck full set consumption due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100g, translating to over 5,000 mg sodium per full set), and should request reduced hoisin sauce and soy-based condiments to lower overall sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat pancakes combined with high fat content from duck skin
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the wheat pancake component which contributes a moderate glycemic load (GL approximately 24 per full set); limiting pancake portions, removing duck skin to reduce saturated fat, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from duck skin and high sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Peking Duck full set sparingly due to its significant saturated fat content (approximately 4.6g per 100g from duck skin) and high sodium load from hoisin sauce and soy-based marinades, both of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peking Duck (Full Set), known in Chinese as 北京烤鸭（整套）(Běijīng Kǎoyā), is one of China's most iconic and celebrated dishes, originating from Beijing and with a culinary history spanning over 600 years. The dish features a whole duck that is meticulously prepared through a multi-stage process: the duck is cleaned, inflated to separate skin from fat, coated with maltose syrup and soy-based marinade, air-dried for 24–48 hours, and then roasted in a hung or closed oven at high temperature until the skin achieves its characteristic deep mahogany colour and exceptional crispness. The full set presentation includes the carved roasted duck served alongside thin, soft Mandarin-style wheat pancakes (薄饼), hoisin sauce (海鲜酱), julienned spring onion, and cucumber strips. Diners wrap slices of crispy duck skin and tender meat with a smear of hoisin sauce and vegetables inside the pancake. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and slightly sweet, with a textural contrast between the shatteringly crisp skin and moist, flavourful meat. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (approximately 280 kcal per 100g composite), high in protein (18.5g per 100g) and fat (16.8g per 100g, including 4.6g saturated fat from duck skin), with moderate carbohydrate from the pancakes (14.2g per 100g composite). Sodium is elevated due to hoisin sauce and soy-based condiments (~420 mg per 100g). Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and moderate glycemic load. In Singapore, Peking Duck is served at Chinese restaurants and is a popular celebratory and banquet dish. The full set at premium restaurants such as See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) is priced at approximately SGD 140, reflecting the labour-intensive preparation and premium whole-duck presentation.

confidence_reason: No exact Peking Duck full set entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite estimate using USDA SR Legacy roasted duck (meat and skin) at 60% and wheat pancake proxy at 40%, with hoisin sauce sodium contribution. GI is analogously estimated from wheat flour flatbread data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary duck component has strong USDA SR Legacy support and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a composite per-100g estimate for the full set edible portion (roasted duck with skin 60% weight fraction, thin wheat pancakes and condiments 40% weight fraction). Individual restaurant preparations will vary. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for roasted duck, and no reliable value for the composite set is available. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1g per 100g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) present in duck fat at trace levels per USDA data; this is not an industrial trans fat. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on the wheat pancake component; the duck meat itself contributes negligible glycemic load. 5. The full set serving size of 1200g is an estimate for a restaurant full-set portion intended for 4–6 people; per-person intake is approximately 200–300g. 6. Halal status is marked false as duck preparation and hoisin sauce ingredients require halal certification verification; consumers should confirm with the specific restaurant. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy roasted duck data adapted for the composite set. 8. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source database for this food.
